The reason I feel lonely isn’t because I’m an introvert or can’t talk to people. I try to connect, but the way people have treated me—friends, family, even those I approached—has left me feeling invisible. No matter how much I try to initiate conversations, people distance themselves later. So, I’ve started staying silent rather than being fake.

Honestly, I don’t enjoy college anymore. I feel like I don’t belong here. My batchmates have friends, go out, have fun together. I’ve tried to be part of that, but I’m never included. It feels like I don’t exist for them. I keep asking myself, why? What did I do wrong?

People say time heals everything. I’ve been waiting for years, but this phase hasn’t passed. I moved on from my past, but the present feels unbearable. Everywhere I look, people are bonding, making memories, and I’m here feeling left out and different.

I want to change, I want to be happy and have genuine friends, but no matter what I do, nothing changes. Why is life so unfair? Why only me? I know I’m not the only one going through this, but this pain feels endless.

If anyone has been through something similar, how did you overcome it? How do you deal with this feeling of being left out? I really need some advice or hope right now.

1)Never disclose your darkest secrets to anyone even when you are too emotional
2) Always keep low expectations from your friends
3) Always give priority to yourself......I was a Yes Man in college and believe me I regretted about it for a long time
4) You will do mistakes, don't be harsh on yourselves rather learn from it.
5) In this world your parents are the only true selfless person who wants to see you succeed much more than themselves.

"Maybe if I just..." IT'S A TRAP!!! That kind off thinking is down playing her mental health, and it becomes a toxic cycle. There are sooo... many options now a days than living at collage: online schooling, community collage, she can even just go part time. My advise is that she take a gap year to focus on her mental health: therapy, meds, yoga, meditation, interpretive dance... Whatever works for her.
If she feels brave enough she can try getting a retail or fast food job. It forces employees to talk to about 100+ people a day. It's a guarantee that she will experience the best and the worst in people with little back lash. She should only take this rough if she can get past the "maybe if I just..." mind trap. If she doesn't get past this people are going to take advantage of her.
It sounds like she has a supportive family that loves her, and knows that life can be hard at times. She needs to remember that its ok to love, and take care of herself.

This year I started studying music at university (UK) and I remember during the first semester, I would always go home every other weekend. I did have similar thoughts to this girl: thought of dropping out, loneliness and crying my literal heart out etc. Reason being is that I’m not very good at making friends and I had countless thoughts of being a failure. During the first half of the second semester, I felt so homesick and lonely to the point where I thought people were judging me on the street and so that resulted in going home for a week. BUT, after that I reached out to some friends I had made and I started to realise that I liked having company and that I wasn’t alone.

Advice for this girl: Dude I know what you’re going through, I have went through it myself. My best advice for you is to socialise with the people in your course. Maybe ask someone after a lecture if they want to go for a coffee or tell your tutor/lecturer about how you’re feeling. Trust me. When I told my friend about my homesickness she said to me that she could’ve helped if I had just said something to her.

But uh, yeah that’s that I guess…

I didn't make any friends because I felt like I couldn't connect with anyone, and I became lonely real quick. I never got close with any of the roomates I had. The only person who gave me the right comfort was my fiancé. My parents didn't understand how I felt and that made me feel like crap. There would be days where I would sit in my dorm room and cry. I felt like I was in hell. But I didn't feel like I needed to drop out and I eventually got my degree in the end because I focused on my studies and my personal writing.

I can't help but feel your video (and its title) treats loneliness like a character flaw instead of a normal human emotion that signals our need for connection. You can have great self-esteem and still feel lonely. It's not about not loving yourself enough, it's about lacking meaningful relationships or community. Loneliness actually serves important purposes: it motivates us to seek genuine connection, provides space for self-reflection, and helps us recognize when our current relationships aren't meeting our needs. The goal shouldn't be eliminating loneliness 'forever' (which is unrealistic anyway), but developing healthy ways to navigate it when it comes and using it as information about what kind of connections we need. Nothing wrong with feeling lonely from time to time.. it's just how you manage that feeling. Loneliness is just another emotion like sadness or anxiety. It's going to come up sometimes, and that's completely normal. The key is learning healthy ways to sit with it, understand what it's telling you, and take appropriate action when needed, rather than treating it like something that needs to be "cured" or avoided at all costs.

When I was younger, I thought confidence was something you either had or didn’t. I thought some women were just born with it – the way they moved, the way they spoke, the way they made men stop and take notice. I thought it was something I’d never have.

Then one day, a friend gave me a copy of The Silent Feminine Power by Lana Cressel. She didn’t say much, just handed it to me with a knowing look. I put it on my shelf and forgot about it.

Months later, after a particularly hard day, I pulled it down and started reading. The words were quiet, but they cut deep. It wasn’t about pretending to be confident. It was about stepping into a space where I didn’t need to prove anything to anyone – not to men, not to my boss, not even to myself.

It was a slow process. There were days when I doubted it was working. But then came the moments – the job interview I nailed without breaking a sweat, the time I walked into a room and felt heads turn without saying a word, the relationship that blossomed because I was finally showing up as my true self.

I used to be like this too, I think I have an undiagnosed neurodivergent condition, when I was in middle school other kids would call me weird and bully me. I had (and still have to some degree) the lowest self esteem imaginable, I couldn't look in the mirror even when I had to brush my hair. The only way I could stand out was to make a fool out of myself, so that's what I was doing to get some attention even if it was at the cost of my dignity.

Highschool I was very depressed, didn't talk to anyone, managed to make like 1 or 2 friends. But at 17 something clicked, it was as if I had enough of it and wanted some change. I knew that people wouldn't approach me, so I went out of my comfort zone and started talking to new people, being as extroverted as possible, and it worked. I developed my sense of humor and kindness in the years prior, so making friends was easy. I now have tons of friends, and my close inner circle is around 5 people besides me. I can easily go talk to somebody, and I don't take it personally anymore. Talking to my friends made me realize that a lot of people just don't take initiative, they may see someone and think they're very cool but they still won't approach that person. Out of the dozens of people I know I'm the only one who does it, so it made me think that it's not actually me who was at fault. I wasn't too ugly, too lame, too whatever. It's just that people lack initiative and are afraid of approaching first.

So don't take it personally, there's nothing wrong with you, and I'm well aware I'm a happy case that was able to change. I still suffer from depression, and I highly suggest getting therapy if possible. If not keep in mind it's not YOU that makes people not approach you, it's other people not taking initiative to do so. I assure you there's been lots of people in your life that you met who thought you were cool af and never approached. And normal is subjective, my close friends are also neurodivergent, and for us this is the normal. Just find your normal, don't try to be something you're not, I assure you there's people just like you who you'd get along with greatly.

1. It's all about showing up for yourself even in the moments when you don't feel like it

3. give yourself the same love and respect you would give to someone you like
4. become the standard you are asking for in other people (friends or partners)

6. be a little delusional
7. once you feel the love you give to yourself you won't have the need to seek validation from other people

A therapist recommended this years ago, and I had the idea of documenting my experience because I knew I'd never want to forget this adventure. I've never felt more grounded and close to myself! Tam, you're a gem. Thank you so much for helping me realize this. I AGREE 100% with everything you said in this video, so true. Loneliness is a feeling of disconnection- that's what it feels like to me, just a feeling of utter disconnection, and this one word-  'disconnected' is what I hear in every person's explanation of what loneliness feels like. I thought I was just disconnected from the world because of my inability to be intimate and vulnerable enough to be my 'true self' to others. No. I was disconnected from MYSELF because of my inability to be intimate and vulnerable enough to even LOOK at my 'true self', know myself, face myself and CONNECT with myself. The world is a mirror. When you have issues with relationships with others, that simply means you have an issue with your relationship with YOURSELF. It is never an external problem, it always stems from the internal world. What I've realized about my constant state of stress and pressure when socializing, and my inability to truly connect and be genuine and form genuine relationships with others, is that it's because I don't even know myself. I can't even allow myself to be my genuine self to myself. I'm afraid to find out something about myself I won't like, and so I hide both from others and from myself. These issues were never about them, it was always about me. But now that I know what the real problem is here, I know what the real solution is, and I can work on it. And that is actually getting to know myself, connecting with myself, and being present and attentive to myself, being present with myself and not some distant 'perfect' future. Thank you so much Tam you really help me and so many people out in a productive and compassionate way, you make me feel seen and like it will all be okay, LOVE YOUUUU (I'm sorry for my neverending saga of me accidentally oversharing on the internet)

This video made me cry because I relate so much. I’m from Brazil and came to Germany to study. Moving to a new country was hard, but I thought university would make things better. At first, I was motivated and hopeful, but over time, loneliness and insecurity took over. Struggling became my “normal,” and I grew numb to being isolated.

I lived in a 7-person flat for almost four years. At first, I didn’t get close to my flatmates, but eventually, we started getting along. Then they moved out, and some self-centered 18-year-olds moved in. They treated the flat like a temporary dorm and didn’t care about the space or the people living there. It was sad, because for me, it was my only home. Over time, the lack of connection and constant indifference made the environment unbearable. Yet, I got so used to it that it started to feel normal, even though it was far from healthy.

At university, things were no different. I didn’t make friends, and loneliness slowly eroded my self-esteem. The longer I was alone, the harder it became to socialize; it felt unnatural. I started believing that life was meant to be this way, that enduring loneliness was a sign of strength. Even when I looked for help, the isolation felt impossible to escape. The university’s cold and bureaucratic system only added to the sense of disconnection.

Eventually, I accepted this life, even though it was making me sick. At first, I was such a different person. I was motivated, tried hard to fit in, attended social events and gatherings, but I slowly stopped trying. Reality hit me hard, and I became someone I never wanted to be: lonely, disconnected, and unable to see a way out. Now, I’m neither happy nor sad, just existing, always alone. But I have hope that I’ll be able to get better.

The longer you’re alone, the harder it gets to reconnect. Loneliness takes something from you; it makes you forget how to connect and how those connections can bring joy and meaning to your life. Over time, it messes with your self-esteem, makes you doubt yourself, neglect your needs, and even believe that life is meant to be this way. It feels as though loneliness rewires your brain, making socializing feel unnatural and draining. Even though I have a few friends, maintaining relationships is exhausting. Everything feels temporary and fragile, as if it could disappear at any moment. This sense of impermanence makes it hard to trust or feel grounded, leaving you stuck in a constant cycle of isolation. What you experience for such a long time shapes the way you think and act, making you unconsciously seek these patterns and believe they are inevitable.

So thank you, Christina. Hearing you share your story made me feel seen and understood.

PARAGRAPH COMMENT ALERT! This video was one of the most validating ones I've watched, and I can attest to most of the things you've spoken about, plus or minus some things - I'm a 3rd year this year and I think I've reached my lowest point this year in this sphere but I've grown a lot since then, and I have to say in many ways this has been 1 of my best years in so many ways, thank God. I've increasingly felt like an outsider for a long time owing to my background, lifestyle, and admittedly the fact that I am pretty weird lol - Also I reckon owing to Covid and other factors I think I'm 2 years behind in terms of social development and I feel this is really my 1st year socially speaking - Ive made countless social mistakes and I can certainly say that loneliness can be a self perpetuating cycle (I realised this year that I had adopted some behaviours that would characteristic of social anxiety as a response to unfortunate social experiences) but I feel like I'm much more aware now and Ive actively started seeking out people that accept me for who I am and are interested in spending time together, and it certainly takes work and planning (you have to actively seek out people you've met that you have mutual respect with and ask them to spend time with you), and having enough respect for yourself to accept what is acceptable/unacceptable to yourself in terms of who you would call a friend - It's not easy to do and Im not where I want to be yet but Ive found 1. trying to challenge your faulty self evaluation (which gets even worse when you feel lonely as you tend to percieve your own actions as worse and other people's actions as rejection rather than acknowledging that there are many external reasons a person may act negatively towards you eg. they're hungry or they've had a difficult day etc.) 2. Remembering that unless a person is interested in you, which is a good sign, most people don't really care about the social mistakes you make, assuming you apologise for any wrongdoing. As far as social relationships go, I think the saying that "the opposite of love is not hate, it's indifference" truly applies 3. Knowing that even if you are socially rejected, the horrible feeling that comes with that is to try and encourage you to find human connection, and you can and will survive it 4. Valuing HONEST, genuine connection over being nice/agreeable is absolutely paramount - it's hard, but politely/respectfully/humbly expressing your feelings and thoughts (the bad as well as the good) to people you feel safe doing so with (and to be honest Im starting to think I should do this with people I don't feel safe with, within reason) may lead to rejection in the short term but in the long term, the people you do form a relationship with will like you for who you are rather than trying to maintain a persona just to be accepted which is exhausting and wont lead to genuine connection even if you do maintain it, of all the lovely people I feel close with, the common factor with all of them is I have told them honestly about myself and I have been honoured by them being honest with me - Thank you again for this video, I'll get off my soapbox now 😅
